ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon or low-carb gyudon with okra and dashi. FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for gyudon or low-carb beef bowl. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. The dish also contains tofu, okra, salad, and dashi sauce which are not captured by this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept specifically covers gyudon or low-carb beef bowl with okra and dashi. No defensible code identified; null assigned.

preparation_method: served over salad and tofu base
allergens: gluten (dashi sauce may contain soy sauce with wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising thinly sliced simmered beef, okra, dashi sauce, a portion of mixed salad greens, and a block of silken tofu served as a low-carbohydrate substitute for steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (USDA SR Legacy 13462) combined with component estimation for tofu, okra, dashi, and salad greens
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 45% thinly sliced simmered beef (USDA SR Legacy), 15% silken tofu (USDA SR Legacy 16126), 10% okra cooked (USDA SR Legacy 11279), 25% mixed salad greens (USDA SR Legacy 11251), and 5% dashi-based sauce (estimated from kombu and bonito dashi, USDA SR Legacy). Macros cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods beef entries. All component sources are USDA SR Legacy or Foundation Foods.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous estimate based on mixed beef and vegetable dish with tofu; reference entry: beef and vegetable stew (GI ~30–35, low category).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for okra low-carb gyudon with dashi sauce. GI estimated at 32 based on the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database: mixed beef and vegetable dish with low-carbohydrate base (GI range 28–38, low category). The rice-free, tofu-and-salad base substantially lowers the GI compared to standard gyudon (which uses steamed rice, GI ~72). Okra contains soluble fibre (mucilage) which further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 14.4) / 100 ≈ 4.6 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dashi sauce and soy-based seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides an estimated 1,178 mg sodium (approximately 310 mg per 100 g × 380 g), which represents roughly 51% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium dashi or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index due to rice-free base and high soluble fibre from okra
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.6 per serving, this dish is a favourable low-carbohydrate option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the okra mucilage and tofu protein further attenuate postprandial blood glucose response, making it a suitable meal choice when sodium is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from dashi-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 7.6 g saturated fat per serving from the beef component, which should be accounted for within the AHA recommended limit of less than 13 g saturated fat per day; the high sodium content also warrants caution for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, and choosing lean beef cuts or reducing sauce volume can improve the cardiovascular profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okra Low Carb Gyudon with Dashi Sauce is a Japanese-style beef bowl dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese fast-casual chain, adapted for low-carbohydrate dietary preferences. Unlike traditional gyudon (牛丼), which is served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain rice, this variant replaces the rice base with a combination of mixed salad greens and silken tofu, substantially reducing the carbohydrate content and glycemic load of the meal. The dish features thinly sliced beef simmered in a savoury dashi-based sauce — a traditional Japanese stock made from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes) — which imparts a characteristic umami depth. Okra is added as a key vegetable component, contributing soluble dietary fibre in the form of mucilage, which has been associated with attenuation of postprandial blood glucose response. The overall flavour profile is savoury and umami-forward, with a mild sweetness from the simmering sauce, a slightly viscous texture from the okra, and a soft, cooling contrast from the tofu. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate meal providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with 9.2 g protein, 5.4 g fat, and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The estimated glycemic index is 32 (low category), making it a clinically relevant option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, contributed by the dashi sauce and soy-based seasoning, which may be relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding lactose, but contains soy and potentially gluten from soy sauce used in the dashi preparation.
common_user_queries: Beef bowl with tofu instead of rice

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for beef, tofu, okra, salad greens, and dashi sauce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the closest comparable mixed beef and vegetable low-carbohydrate dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese fast-casual beef bowl portions. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 (sentinel value for missing data): vitamin D is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component sources used for this dish (SR Legacy entries 13462, 16126, 11279, 11251 do not include reliable vitamin D values for these cooked components); it is therefore marked as missing data per G3 sentinel-value policy. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives', which accurately reflects the primary composition of this dish (beef, tofu, okra). 6. Allergen status for gluten is flagged as uncertain because commercial dashi sauce and soy sauce used in restaurant preparation typically contain wheat; individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity should confirm with the restaurant. 7. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore's halal certification status should be verified independently.
